subject: Physics
q: A hoop of radius <math> <mrow> <mtext>r</mtext> </mrow> </math> and mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math> rotating with an angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is placed on a rough horizontal surface. The initial velocity of the centre of the hoop is zero. What will be the velocity of the centre of the hoop when it ceases to slip?
question: A hoop of radius <math> <mrow> <mtext>r</mtext> </mrow> </math> and mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math> rotating with an angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is placed on a rough horizontal surface. The initial velocity of the centre of the hoop is zero. What will be the velocity of the centre of the hoop when it ceases to slip?
solution:
By conservation of angular momentum at the point of contact 
 
mr2ω0=mvr+mr2ω=mvr+mr2.v/r
⇒  r2ω0=2vr⇒v=rω0/2

subject: Physics
q: The moment of inertia of a square loop made of four uniform solid cylinders, each having radius $R$ and length $L(\mathrm{R}<\mathrm{L})$ about an axis passing through the mid points of opposite sides, is (Take the mass of the entire loop as $M$) :
question: The moment of inertia of a square loop made of four uniform solid cylinders, each having radius $R$ and length $L(\mathrm{R}<\mathrm{L})$ about an axis passing through the mid points of opposite sides, is (Take the mass of the entire loop as $M$) :
solution:
Each cylinder has mass $M/4$, radius $R$, length $L$.
Axis through midpoints of top and bottom sides (along y-direction).
Top/bottom cylinders (perpendicular to axis, axis through midpoint):
$I = \frac{M_c R^2}{4} + \frac{M_c L^2}{12}$ each.
Left/right cylinders (parallel to axis, distance $L/2$ from it):
$I = \frac{M_c R^2}{2} + M_c(L/2)^2$ each.
Total $= 2\left(\frac{M_c R^2}{4}+\frac{M_c L^2}{12}\right) + 2\left(\frac{M_c R^2}{2}+\frac{M_c L^2}{4}\right)$
$= \frac{M}{2}\left(\frac{3R^2}{4}+\frac{L^2}{3}\right) = \frac{3MR^2}{8}+\frac{ML^2}{6}$.

q:
Electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave propagating through a non-magnetic medium is given by E=20cos2×1010t-200x V m-1. The dielectric constant of the medium is equal to:
(Take μr=1 )
question:
Electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave propagating through a non-magnetic medium is given by E=20cos2×1010t-200x V m-1. The dielectric constant of the medium is equal to:
(Take μr=1 )
solution:
Equate the wave equation with the equation of EM wave, E=E0cosωt-kx V m-1 here the given equation of EM wave,  E=20cos2×1010t-200x V m-1⇒v=ωk=2×1010200=108 m s-1
The refractive index,⇒n=cv=3×108108⇒3=μrεr  μr=1given)
εr=9

subject: Physics
q: ICM is moment of inertia of a circular disc about an axis (CM) passing through its center and perpendicular to the plane of disc. IAB is its moment of inertia about an axis AB perpendicular to plane and parallel to axis CM at a distance 23R from center, where R is the radius of the disc. The ratio of IAB and ICM is x:9. The value of x is ______.
question: ICM is moment of inertia of a circular disc about an axis (CM) passing through its center and perpendicular to the plane of disc. IAB is its moment of inertia about an axis AB perpendicular to plane and parallel to axis CM at a distance 23R from center, where R is the radius of the disc. The ratio of IAB and ICM is x:9. The value of x is ______.
solution:
We have,  ICM=12MR2 

Since AB is parallel to diameter and at a distance of 23R from it, using parallel axis theorem,

IAB=mR22+m2R32=1718mR2

Thus, the ratio is 

∴IABICM=1718MR212MR2=179

Thus, x=17

subject: Physics
q: Four identical discs each of mass 'M' and diameter 'a' are arranged in a small plane as shown in figure. If the moment of inertia of the system about OO' is x4Ma2. Then, the value of x will be _____ .
question: Four identical discs each of mass 'M' and diameter 'a' are arranged in a small plane as shown in figure. If the moment of inertia of the system about OO' is x4Ma2. Then, the value of x will be _____ .
solution:
Considering four identical discs as 1, 2, 3 and 4.

Moment of inertia of disc about diagonal is I1=I3=MR24

Now using parallel axis theorem, 

Moment of inertia of disc 2 and 4 is 

I2=I4=MR24+MR2=54MR2

So, net moment of inertia about OO' is 

 I=I1+I2+I3+I4

=2MR24+254MR2

 =MR22+52MR2=3MR2

Putting R=a2

I=3Ma24, so,x=3

q: The radius of gyration of a uniform rod of length <math><mi>l</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> about an axis passing through a point <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> away from the centre of the rod, and perpendicular to it, is:
question: The radius of gyration of a uniform rod of length <math><mi>l</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> about an axis passing through a point <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> away from the centre of the rod, and perpendicular to it, is:
solution:
Ml212+Ml42=MK2
l212+l216=K2
K=748l

subject: Physics
q: A person of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> mass is standing on the rim of a circular platform of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> rotating about its axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> revolutions per minute (rpm). The person now starts moving towards the centre of the platform. What will be the rotational speed (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rpm</mi></math> ) of the platform when the person reaches its centre....
question: A person of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> mass is standing on the rim of a circular platform of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> rotating about its axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> revolutions per minute (rpm). The person now starts moving towards the centre of the platform. What will be the rotational speed (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rpm</mi></math> ) of the platform when the person reaches its centre....
solution:
Applying conservation of angular momentum,

mR2+MR22ω=MR22ω'

80+2002×5=2002ω'

ω'=9rev/sec.

The free end is held vertically above the pivot and then released. The angular acceleration of the rod when it makes an angle θ with the vertical is:
question: A slender uniform rod of mass M and length l is pivoted at one end so that it can rotate in a vertical plane (see figure). There is negligible friction at the pivot. The free end is held vertically above the pivot and then released. The angular acceleration of the rod when it makes an angle θ with the vertical is:
solution:
Moment of a force about O is lα=mgl2sinθ

⇒α=mgl2ml23sinθ

⇒α=3g2lsin⁡θ

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere with uniform density and radius R is rotating initially with constant angular velocity $\left(\omega_1\right)$ about its diameter. After some time during the rotation its starts loosing mass at a uniform rate, with no change in its shape. The angular velocity of the sphere when its radius become $R / 2$ is $x \omega_1$. The value of $x$ is ________
question: A solid sphere with uniform density and radius R is rotating initially with constant angular velocity $\left(\omega_1\right)$ about its diameter. After some time during the rotation its starts loosing mass at a uniform rate, with no change in its shape. The angular velocity of the sphere when its radius become $R / 2$ is $x \omega_1$. The value of $x$ is ________
solution:
When sphere is of radius $R$, its mass is $M$, when radius is reduced to $\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}$, mass will reduced to $\frac{\mathrm{M}}{8}$ Now by conservation of angular momentum
$\begin{aligned}
& \left(\tau_{e x t}=0\right) \\ & L_1=L_2 \\ & I_1 \omega_1=I_2 \omega_2 \\ & \left(\frac{2}{5} M R^2\right) \omega_1=\left(\frac{2}{5}\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{8}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}\right)^2\right) \omega_2
\end{aligned}$
$\omega_2=32 \omega_1$ value of x is 32
Answer is 32

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Moment of inertia of a circular disc of mass M and radius R about X,Y axes (passing through its plane) and Z-axis which is perpendicular to its plane were found to be Ix,Iy and Iz, respectively. The respective radii of gyration about all the three axes will be the same.

Reason R: A rigid body making rotational motion has fixed mass and shape. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Moment of inertia of a circular disc of mass M and radius R about X,Y axes (passing through its plane) and Z-axis which is perpendicular to its plane were found to be Ix,Iy and Iz, respectively. The respective radii of gyration about all the three axes will be the same.

Reason R: A rigid body making rotational motion has fixed mass and shape. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both A and R are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is not correct but R is correct.
A is correct but R is not correct.

Both A and R are correct and R is the correct
explanation of A.
solution:
Iz=Ix+Iy (using perpendicular axis theorem)

and I=mk2(K: radius of gyration) 

so mKz2=mKx2+mKy2

Kz2=Kx2+Ky2

So radius of gyration about axes x,y and z will not be same, hence assertion A is not correct reason R is correct statement (property of a rigid body).

q: A body rotating with an angular speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rpm</mi></math> is uniformly accelerated to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rpm</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sec</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The number of rotations made in the process is
question: A body rotating with an angular speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rpm</mi></math> is uniformly accelerated to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rpm</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sec</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The number of rotations made in the process is
solution:
ωf=ω0+αt

α=1200×6

θ=ω0t+12αt2

=600×1060+12×1200×6×136

θ=200

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass m=1 kg slides with velocity v=6 m s-1 on a frictionless horizontal surface and collides with a uniform vertical rod and sticks to it as shown. The rod is pivoted about O and swings as a result of the collision making angle θ before momentarily coming to rest. if the rod has mass M=2 kg, and length ℓ=1 m, the value of θ is approximately(take g=10 m s-2
question: A block of mass m=1 kg slides with velocity v=6 m s-1 on a frictionless horizontal surface and collides with a uniform vertical rod and sticks to it as shown. The rod is pivoted about O and swings as a result of the collision making angle θ before momentarily coming to rest. if the rod has mass M=2 kg, and length ℓ=1 m, the value of θ is approximately(take g=10 m s-2
solution:
Angular momentum

mvl=ml2+2ml23ω

mvl=53ml2ω

ω=3v5l

12Iω2=2mgl21-cos θ+mgl1-cos θ

1253ml29v225l2=2mgl 1-cos θ

35×2mv2=2mgl1-cosθ

310×362×10=1-cos θ

1-2750=cos θ

cos θ=2350=0.46

θ=cos-10.46=63°

subject: Physics
q:
A uniform disc with mass M=4 kg and radius R=10 cm is mounted on a fixed horizontal axle as shown in figure. A block with mass m=2 kg hangs from a massless cord that is wrapped around the rim of the disc. During the fall of the block, the cord does not slip and there is no friction at the axle. The tension in the cord is _____ N.

(Take g=10 ms-2)
question:
A uniform disc with mass M=4 kg and radius R=10 cm is mounted on a fixed horizontal axle as shown in figure. A block with mass m=2 kg hangs from a massless cord that is wrapped around the rim of the disc. During the fall of the block, the cord does not slip and there is no friction at the axle. The tension in the cord is _____ N.

(Take g=10 ms-2)
solution:
Let a be the acceleration of block and α be the angular acceleration of disc.

Now, for unwrapping without slipping, Rα=a.

Forces acting on block are shown in FDB.

Using Newton's second law

mg-T=ma   ...1

Torque about centre of disc is τ=Ia

TR=MR22α=4R22α=⇒T=2Rα   ...2

Putting the value of tension in equation 1, we get

mg-2Rα=ma⇒mg-2RaR=ma⇒mg-2a=ma⇒ma+2a=mg⇒a=mgm+2=2×102+2=5 m s-2

Now, α=50.1=50 rad s-2

Tension in the cord is T=2×0.1×50=10 N.

subject: Physics
q: A hollow sphere is rolling on a plane surface about its axis of symmetry. The ratio of rotational kinetic energy to its total kinetic energy is $\frac{x}{5}$. The value of $x$ is _____ .
question: A hollow sphere is rolling on a plane surface about its axis of symmetry. The ratio of rotational kinetic energy to its total kinetic energy is $\frac{x}{5}$. The value of $x$ is _____ .
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I} \omega^2}{\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I} \omega^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2}=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{mR}^2\right) \omega^2}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{mR}^2\right) \omega^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}(\mathrm{R} \omega)^2} \\ & =\frac{\frac{2}{3}}{\frac{2}{3}+1}=\frac{2}{5} \\ & \mathrm{x}=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Three equal masses $m$ are kept at vertices (A, B, C) of an equilateral triangle of side a in free space. At $t=0$, they are given an initial velocity $\vec{V}_A=V_0 \overrightarrow{A C}, \vec{V}_B=V_0 \overrightarrow{B A}$ and $\vec{V}_C=V_0 \overrightarrow{C B}$. Here, $\overrightarrow{A C}, \overrightarrow{C B}$ and $\overrightarrow{B A}$ are unit vectors along the edges of the triangle. If the three masses interact gravitationally, then the magnitude of the net angular momentum of the system at the point of collision is :
question: Three equal masses $m$ are kept at vertices (A, B, C) of an equilateral triangle of side a in free space. At $t=0$, they are given an initial velocity $\vec{V}_A=V_0 \overrightarrow{A C}, \vec{V}_B=V_0 \overrightarrow{B A}$ and $\vec{V}_C=V_0 \overrightarrow{C B}$. Here, $\overrightarrow{A C}, \overrightarrow{C B}$ and $\overrightarrow{B A}$ are unit vectors along the edges of the triangle. If the three masses interact gravitationally, then the magnitude of the net angular momentum of the system at the point of collision is :
solution: $d=\frac{a}{2 \sqrt{3}}$ Angular momentum of one mass about point $O$ $\begin{aligned} L & =m v d \\ & =m v_0 \cdot \frac{a}{2 \sqrt{3}} \end{aligned}$ Net angular momentum about point $O$ $\begin{aligned} L_{\text {net }} & =32 \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{3} m v_0 a}{2} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A cubical block of side 30 cm is moving with velocity 2 m s-1 on a smooth horizontal surface. The surface has a bump at a point O as shown in the figure. The angular velocity (in rad/s) of the block immediately after it hits the bump, is :
question: A cubical block of side 30 cm is moving with velocity 2 m s-1 on a smooth horizontal surface. The surface has a bump at a point O as shown in the figure. The angular velocity (in rad/s) of the block immediately after it hits the bump, is :
solution:
Conservation of angular momentum about point O,
Lbefore=Lafter
⇒mvR=Iω
⇒ω=m×2×a2m a26+a22
⇒ω=128a=32×0.3=102=5 rad s-1

subject: Physics
q: A pulley of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is rotated about its axis by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>12</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> applied tangentially (while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is measured in seconds). If moment of inertia of the pulley about its axis of rotation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, the number of rotations made by the pulley before its direction of motion is reversed, will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>K</mi><mi>π</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A pulley of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is rotated about its axis by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>12</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> applied tangentially (while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is measured in seconds). If moment of inertia of the pulley about its axis of rotation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, the number of rotations made by the pulley before its direction of motion is reversed, will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>K</mi><mi>π</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Torque of pulley about its axis is τ=Iα, where, I is moment of inertia and α is angular acceleration.

Torque of a force about its axis is τ=FR, here, R is radius of pulley.

Then, 12t-3t21.5=4.5α 

⇒α=4t-t2 

Angular acceleration in terms of angular velocity is

 ⇒α=dωdt=4t-t2

⇒ω=∫0t4t-t2dt

⇒ω=2t2-t33

For ω=0, 2t2-t33=0⇒t22-t3=0

 ⇒t=0 or 6 s

Now, using ω=dθdt, we have

dθdt=2t2-t33⇒θ=∫062t2-t33dt

=2t33-t41206 

=6323-612=638-612=636=36

Number of revolutions =362π=18π

∴K=18

subject: Physics
q:
An object of mass 8 kg is hanging from one end of a uniform rod CD of mass 2 kg and length 1 m pivoted at its end C on a vertical wall as shown in figure. It is supported by a cable AB such that the system is in equilibrium. The tension in the cable is:

(Take g=10 m s-2)
question:
An object of mass 8 kg is hanging from one end of a uniform rod CD of mass 2 kg and length 1 m pivoted at its end C on a vertical wall as shown in figure. It is supported by a cable AB such that the system is in equilibrium. The tension in the cable is:

(Take g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Let T be the tension in cable.

Since the system is at equilibrium, the net torque, about an axis perpendicular to the plane and passing through the point of contact of the rod and the wall, is zero. Therefore, torque about point C

T sin30°× 60=mg×50+8g×100

T2×60=20×50+80×100

⇒3T=100+800

⇒300 N.

question: A force of 40 N acts on a point B at the end of an L-shaped object as shown in the figure. The angle θ that will produce the maximum moment of the force about point A is given by:

subject: Physics
q: A uniform bar of length 12 cm and mass $20 m$ lies on a smooth horizontal table. Two point masses $m$ and $2 m$ are moving in opposite directions with same speed of $v$ and in the same plane as the bar, as shown in figure. These masses strike the bar simultaneously and get stuck to it. After collision the entire system is rotating with angular frequency $\omega$. The ratio of $v$ and $\omega$ is :
question: A uniform bar of length 12 cm and mass $20 m$ lies on a smooth horizontal table. Two point masses $m$ and $2 m$ are moving in opposite directions with same speed of $v$ and in the same plane as the bar, as shown in figure. These masses strike the bar simultaneously and get stuck to it. After collision the entire system is rotating with angular frequency $\omega$. The ratio of $v$ and $\omega$ is :
solution:
Linear momentum conservation: $mv - 2mv = 23m \cdot v_{cm}$, giving $v_{cm} = -\frac{v}{23}$.
Angular momentum about center of mass from the collision geometry:
Taking collision points 2 cm and 4 cm from the 12 cm bar's center at different positions, the angular momentum is $L = m \cdot v \cdot r_1 + (-2m) \cdot v \cdot r_2$.
Calculating moment of inertia: $I = \frac{1}{12}(20m)(0.12)^2 + m(0.02)^2 + 2m(0.04)^2 = 0.0276m$
The angular frequency $\omega$ is obtained from $\omega = \frac{L}{I}$, giving ratio $\frac{v}{\omega} = 33$.

subject: Physics
q: The torque due to the force $(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})$ about the origin, acting on a particle whose position vector is $(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$, would be
question: The torque due to the force $(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})$ about the origin, acting on a particle whose position vector is $(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$, would be
solution: $\begin{aligned} \tau & =\dot{r} \times \dot{F} \\ \tau & =\left|\begin{array}{lll}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 2\end{array}\right| \\ \tau & =\hat{i}(2-1)-\hat{j}[0]+\hat{k}(1-2) \\ & =\hat{i}-\hat{k}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The moment of inertia of a uniform thin rod about a perpendicular axis passing through one end is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. The same rod is bent into a ring and its moment of inertia about a diameter is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be ______.
question: The moment of inertia of a uniform thin rod about a perpendicular axis passing through one end is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. The same rod is bent into a ring and its moment of inertia about a diameter is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be ______.
solution:
Moment of inertia of rod rotating about an axis passing through one end is I1=ml23     ⋯1 

Moment of inertia of ring rotating about one of its diameters is I2=mR22.

Since the rod is bent into a ring, the circumference of the ring and the length of the rod are equal, l=2πR

Then, I2=mR22=ml28π2 

Thus, the ratio I1I2=8π23, so x=8.

subject: Physics
q: When the position vector $\vec{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}$ changes sign as $-\vec{r}$, which one of the following vector will not flip under sign change ?
question: When the position vector $\vec{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}$ changes sign as $-\vec{r}$, which one of the following vector will not flip under sign change ?
solution:
The transformation $\vec{r} \to -\vec{r}$ is known as a parity transformation (inversion through the origin).
Vectors that change sign under parity are called polar vectors, while those that do not change sign are called axial vectors (or pseudovectors).
Velocity is defined as $\vec{v} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$. Since $\vec{r} \to -\vec{r}$, we have $\vec{v} \to -\vec{v}$.
Acceleration is defined as $\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$. Since $\vec{v} \to -\vec{v}$, we have $\vec{a} \to -\vec{a}$.
Linear momentum is defined as $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$. Since $\vec{v} \to -\vec{v}$, we have $\vec{p} \to -\vec{p}$.
Angular momentum is defined as $\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p}$.
Under the transformation $\vec{r} \to -\vec{r}$ and $\vec{p} \to -\vec{p}$, the cross product becomes:
$\vec{L}' = (-\vec{r}) \times (-\vec{p}) = \vec{r} \times \vec{p} = \vec{L}$.
Thus, angular momentum does not flip its sign under the transformation $\vec{r} \to -\vec{r}$.

subject: Physics
q: If force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> acts on a particle having position vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> then, the torque about the origin will be:
question: If force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> acts on a particle having position vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> then, the torque about the origin will be:
solution:
Torque in vector form is expressed as vector multiplication of position vector and force.

τ→=r→×F→

⇒τ→=i^j^k^21234-2

⇒τ→=-2-8i^--4-6j^+8-3k^

⇒τ→=-10i^+10j^+5k^ N m

subject: Physics
q: A ring of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is rotating about its axis with angular velocity $\omega$. Two identical bodies each of mass $m$ are now gently attached at the two ends of a diameter of the ring. Because of this, the kinetic energy loss will be :
question: A ring of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is rotating about its axis with angular velocity $\omega$. Two identical bodies each of mass $m$ are now gently attached at the two ends of a diameter of the ring. Because of this, the kinetic energy loss will be :
solution:
Kinetic energy $_{\text {(rotational) }} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I} \omega^2$
Kinetic energy $_{\text {(translational) }} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Mv}^2$
$\begin{aligned}
& (\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{R} \omega) \\
& \text { M.I. }_{\text {(initial) }} \mathrm{I}_{\text {ring }}=\mathrm{MR}^2 ; \omega_{\text {initial }}=\omega \\
& \text { M.I. } _{\text { (new) }}{\mathrm{I}^{\prime}}{ }_{\text {(system) }}=\mathrm{MR}^2+2 \mathrm{mR}^2 \\
& \omega_{(\text {system) }}^{\prime}=\frac{M \omega}{M+2 m} \\
&
\end{aligned}$
Solving we get loss in $\mathrm{K.E.}$
$=\frac{\mathrm{Mm}}{(\mathrm{M}+2 \mathrm{~m})} \omega^2 \mathrm{R}^2$

subject: Physics
q:
Consider a badminton racket with length scales as shown in the figure.

If the mass of the linear and circular portions of the badminton racket are same (M) and the mass of the threads are negligible, the moment of inertia of the racket about an axis perpendicular to the handle and in the plane of the ring at, r2 distance from the end A of the handle will be ______Mr2.
question:
Consider a badminton racket with length scales as shown in the figure.

If the mass of the linear and circular portions of the badminton racket are same (M) and the mass of the threads are negligible, the moment of inertia of the racket about an axis perpendicular to the handle and in the plane of the ring at, r2 distance from the end A of the handle will be ______Mr2.
solution:
IA=Mr22+M7r−r22+M(6r)212+M3r−r22

=Mr22+M(169)r24+3Mr2+25Mr24=171Mr24+37Mr24=2084Mr2=52Mr2

subject: Physics
q: A pulley of radius $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is rotated about its axis by a force $\mathrm{F}=\left(20 \mathrm{t}-5 \mathrm{t}^2\right)$ Newton (where $\mathrm{t}$ is measured in seconds) applied tangentially. If the moment of inertia of the pulley about its axis of rotation made by the pulley before its direction of motion if reversed, is :
question: A pulley of radius $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is rotated about its axis by a force $\mathrm{F}=\left(20 \mathrm{t}-5 \mathrm{t}^2\right)$ Newton (where $\mathrm{t}$ is measured in seconds) applied tangentially. If the moment of inertia of the pulley about its axis of rotation made by the pulley before its direction of motion if reversed, is :
solution:
$\mathrm{r} \times \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{l} \times \alpha$
$2\left(20 \mathrm{t}-5 \mathrm{t}^2\right)=10 \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha=4 \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^2$
$\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{dt}}=4 \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^2$
$\mathrm{~d} \omega=\left(4 \mathrm{t}^2-\mathrm{t}^2\right) \mathrm{dt}$
$\omega=2 \mathrm{t}^2-\frac{\mathrm{t}^3}{3}$ (on integration)
$\omega=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=6 \mathrm{~s}$

subject: Physics
q: A slab is subjected to two forces $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{1}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{2}}$ of same magnitude $F$ as shown in the figure. Force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{2}}$ is in XY- plane while force $\mathrm{F}_{1}$ acts along $z$ -axis at the point $(2 \vec{i}+3 \vec{j})$. The moment of these forces about point $\mathrm{O}$ will be:
question: A slab is subjected to two forces $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{1}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{2}}$ of same magnitude $F$ as shown in the figure. Force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{2}}$ is in XY- plane while force $\mathrm{F}_{1}$ acts along $z$ -axis at the point $(2 \vec{i}+3 \vec{j})$. The moment of these forces about point $\mathrm{O}$ will be:
solution:
Given, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{2}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}})+\frac{\mathrm{F} \sqrt{3}}{2}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{1}=0 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$

Torque due to $\mathrm{F}_{1}$ force

$$

\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{F}_{1}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{1} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{1}=6 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \times\left(\frac{\mathrm{F}}{2}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}})+\frac{\mathrm{F} \sqrt{3}}{2}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})\right)=3 \mathrm{~F}(\hat{\mathrm{k}})

$$

Torque due to $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ force

$$

\begin{aligned}

\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{F}_{2}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \times & \mathrm{F} \hat{\mathrm{k}}=3 \mathrm{~F} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \mathrm{~F}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}}) \\

\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{net}}=\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{F}_{1}}+\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{F}_{2}} &=3 \mathrm{Fi}+2 \mathrm{~F}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})+3 \mathrm{~F}(\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\

&=(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \mathrm{F}

\end{aligned}

$$

subject: Physics
q: A wheel is rolling on a plane surface. The speed of a particle on the highest point of the rim is $8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The speed of the particle on the rim of the wheel at the same level as the centre of wheel, will be :
question: A wheel is rolling on a plane surface. The speed of a particle on the highest point of the rim is $8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The speed of the particle on the rim of the wheel at the same level as the centre of wheel, will be :
solution:
If $V_B=2 V$
Point A is instantaneous center of rotation
Given $V_B=8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{V}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{p}}=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{v} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{p}}=4 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \operatorname{correct}(1)
\end{aligned}$

q: Seven identical circular planar disks, each of mass M and radius R are welded symmetrically as shown. The moment of inertia of the arrangement about the axis normal to the plane and passing through the point P is:
question: Seven identical circular planar disks, each of mass M and radius R are welded symmetrically as shown. The moment of inertia of the arrangement about the axis normal to the plane and passing through the point P is:
solution:
The motion of inertia of the system about O,

12MR2+6×12MR2+6×M×2R2

⇒   I1=72MR2+24MR2

The motion of inertia of the system about P, 

⇒   I1+7MD2

⇒    72MR2+24MR2+7M×3R2

⇒   1812MR2

subject: Physics
q: Four particles, each of mass 1 kg are placed at four corners of a square of side 2 m. The moment of inertia of the system about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through one of its vertex is ______ kg m2.
question: Four particles, each of mass 1 kg are placed at four corners of a square of side 2 m. The moment of inertia of the system about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through one of its vertex is ______ kg m2.
solution:
The formula to calculate the moment of inertia of a system of particles about a point can be written as

I=∑i=1nmiri2   ...1

where, mi is the mass of the ith particle situated at a distance ri from the point.

With reference to the above diagram, it can be written that the net moment of inertia of the system of particles is

I=ma2+ma2+m(2a)2=4ma2=4×1 kg×2 m2=16 kg m2

subject: Physics
q: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached to a string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The ball is rotated on a horizontal circular path about its vertical axis. The maximum tension that the string can bear is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The maximum possible value of angular velocity of the ball in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is,:
question: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached to a string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The ball is rotated on a horizontal circular path about its vertical axis. The maximum tension that the string can bear is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The maximum possible value of angular velocity of the ball in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is,:
solution:
Let the string makes angle θ with the vertical. Weight will be balanced by the vertical component of tension, which is Tcosθ=mg and the horizontal component towards the centre will provide the required centripetal force for circular motion. Therefore,

Tsinθ=mω2lsinθ, where l=length of the string.

⇒400sinθ=0.5ω2×0.5sinθ

⇒ω=40 rad s-1
